○ Primary purpose
■ The Music program at College of the Siskiyous offers degrees, certificates,
and coursework in the areas of vocal performance, instrumental
performance, and music and theater performance. It provides opportunities
in which students develop both the academic and practical skills necessary
to prepare for one of the many careers in music; broaden their general
knowledge of music; develop performance skills or pursue an advanced
degree in music.
■ Degree specific courses offered include music theory, ear training/aural
comprehension, piano, and a multitude of instrumental and vocal
ensembles focusing on classical, jazz, musical theater, and community
music. Additionally, multiple General Education music courses are offered
to both music degree, and non-music degree seeking COS students.
■ Program Learning Outcomes (from Course Catalogue) - Upon successful
completion of this program, the student should be able to:
● Demonstrate an awareness of the ways in which people through the
ages and in different cultures have responded to themselves and the
world around them in artistic and cultural creation.
● Demonstrate the ability to analyze and appreciate works of
philosophical, historical, literary, aesthetic, and cultural
importance.
● Demonstrate the ability to play piano at a grade-three level.
● Analyze works in traditional and non-traditional contexts.
● Compose or arrange works in traditional and non-traditional
contexts.
● Demonstrate the ability to sing at sight and transcribe at hearing
traditional and non-traditional music.
● In the area of chosen focus, demonstrate proficiency at a
comparable level to a student entering upper division coursework.
● Demonstrate an understanding of the responsibilities of
musicianship.

● External Factors with Significant Impact: What external factors have a significant impact
on your program? Please include the following as appropriate:
○ Budgetary constraints or opportunities
■ College of the Siskiyous faces similar budget constraints as other
Community Colleges, however, they are exacerbated by the low socio-
economic status of the region, and consequently, lower tax revenue.
■ Due to a restructuring of department budgets, all music department line
items have been zeroed out with the exception of Instructional Supplies
and Adjunct/EIA. While CQIPs to restore budgets have been submitted for
the 18-19, and 19-20 SY to restore budgets, only one item has any money
allocated (equipment repair, inst) with $250.
■ Because of line items being eliminated, there are many opportunities for
growth in the budget department. In the meantime, music instructors are
minimizing purchases and repairs despite a backlog of needed equipment
and instrument/equipment repairs. Additionally, they are programming
literature from the public domain and/or selecting literature from the COS
Music Department Library.
○ Competition from other institutions

-- 67 of 98 --

title: Microsoft Word - Comprehensive Program Review 2019.docx (Part 21)
text:
68
■ Faculty and Staff - We continue to be at a disadvantage when it comes to
faculty and staff. We produce concerts and events that are on par with
community colleges of any size and any location across the state.
However, there is a cost to the students, faculty, and staff. A review of the
data provided in this report suggests that there has been an increase in FTE
since the hiring of a second full-time instructor. However, one need only
visit websites of other community colleges in California that produce the
number and quality of performances that are presented at COS to realize
that we as an institution are understaffed. While some would call what we
do ‘working efficiently, it is typically at the expense of faculty, staff, and
student health and well-being.
■ Facilities - With a 50 year old facility, we constantly experience
maintenance related issues. Additionally, any infrastructure is antiquated,
and any modifications on a building of this age are putting a band-aid on a
broken arm. While we are on the Governor’s list for a building renovation,
we are years away from completion assuming all contingencies are met
and construction is approved. In the meantime, Napa College and Folsom
Lake College boast recently built $60 million+ Performing Arts
Complexes, and Woodland Community College is slated to break ground
on a $47 million culinary and performing arts facility. If we expect to
compete statewide as a music department, our $27 million renovation is
still a far cry from those being installed on campuses around the state.
■ Local Resources - because of our remote location, we are one illness, one
injury, and one departure away from having to start over. There is no
‘bench’ in Siskiyou County. The EIAs we currently use are essentially the
only EIAs available. We are fortunate that they are qualified to teach at the
collegiate level. However, they are the only ones we have. Any loss in
faculty or instructional staff would result in a major modification to the
entire program. Additionally, while our community is very supportive in
spirit, due to the low socio-economic state of Siskiyou County, the ability
to access local tax revenue, find local individuals who can create
endowments, and build any kind of meaningful/steady funding streams
amplify the impact of internal budget issues.
○ Requirements of four-year institutions
■ After an in depth exploration of music degree requirements of four-year
California Colleges, the two full-time instructors restructured the local
COS AA Degree requirements. By doing this, Music Majors at COS can
complete their degree in two years, and the program more accurately
reflects the courses and rigor that are expected in a four year college.
Additionally, since the most recent Program Review, COS has

higher. Our goal is to keep retention between 85% and 95% for the
next four years.

that we don’t have a semester below 85%.
■ Use the data provided by the Research Office to discuss your FT/PT
Faculty Ratio and how it is impacting your program and/or student

● In the 2015-16 SY there was only one full-time music instructor,
with multiple adjunct instructors covering classes. In the 2016-17
SY an interim full time instructor was hired, which brought some
stability. In the 2017-18 SY a full time tenure track music
instructor was hired, creating greater stability and trust within the
community. Over the course of the past four years, total music
students enrolled grew from 472 in fall 2015/spring 2016 to 615
in fall 2018/spring 2019, generating an increase of 143 students
enrolled in music classes over the course of those years.

In an effort to reestablish a strong connection with the community, and to stabilize the music
department after a long period of instability, the COS music department has made great strides in

-- 94 of 98 --

title: Microsoft Word - Comprehensive Program Review 2019.docx (Part 48)
text:
95
rebuilding its presence as an integral part of the greater community. This has been accomplished
through engagement and course offerings. COS Music Department has strived to offer courses in
a wide variety of formats as well as concert/workshops/clinics/events in various locations across
Siskiyou County. We have offered our bi-annual Concert Band/Choirs & Jazz Ensembles
concerts in both Weed and Yreka along with performances Mt Shasta, Grenada, Etna, Dunsmuir,
and McCloud. COS has created and hosted the Siskiyou County Choral Festivalfor for the 4th
year which draws 300-400 choir students (Grades 4-12). We have also offered our non-music
major general education course (Music Appreciation, Survey of Jazz and Popular Music,
Diversity in American Music) in face-to-face, hybrid, fully online modalities. We have
collaborated with COS Athletics, COS Theater, COS Visual Arts, COS ASB, COS Guidance and
Counselling, Department Yreka High School, Mt Shasta High School, Sisson School, Weed
Elementary & High School, McCloud Elementary, on productions, Butteville Elementary,
Jackson Street Elementary, Gazelle Elementary, City of Mt Shasta, Music Mountain, Red Scarf
Society, collaborative work with church choirs throughout the county on events, and concerts.
The music faculty serve on COS committees which include: Academic Senate, Faculty Union,
Distance Learning Committee, Flex Committee, Creative Spark, International Committee,
Mexico Exchange Committee, Foundation Board, Enrollment Management, Diversity
Committee, various hiring committees and tenure committees, faculty evaluation, graduation
planning, Ford 50th Anniversary Celebration Committee, and COS 8th grade day. Due to the
small faculty size (and degree requirements) we also heavily rely on EIA’s for our ensembles,
musical production, applied music courses. Without these highly trained individuals we could
not impact such a wide variety of courses and events at COS. But we still struggle with a budget
that does not reflect our department's unique offerings on campus. We look forward to the
potential COS Ford Theater/McCloud Hall construction project being funded. With these
facility/technology upgrades along with a central building shared space with Visual arts we can
see new potentially creative collaborations which will help create a stronger campus community
with traditional and life-long learners.

from now? Dream big while considering any upcoming changes (e.g., new buildings,
labs, growth, changes in the discipline, etc.)
Our goal in the next four years is to develop pathways for music/music theater/theater education
majors. We hope to involve our students in undergraduate research, practicum experiences,
leading community music events. We also strive to create skill’s certificates in areas of Private
Instruction, Piano, Band, Jazz, Choir, Chamber Music, Vocal Jazz, composition/arranging,
community music, music education, music therapy and music technology. We also want to
expand our K-12 festival opportunities on campus matching the success of the Siskiyou Choral
Festival. Other areas of growth would be recruiting trips with COS Vocal Music, Instrumental

● Progress on Prior Goals: Briefly summarize any progress your unit has made in meeting
the goals and objectives identified in the program’s last Four-Year Action Plan.
○ We do not have access to the past Four-Year Action Plan. Both full-time music
instructors were not employees during this past plan.
● Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (Goals,
Objectives, Resources, and Actions): Reflect on your responses to all the previous
questions. Write a Four-Year Action Plan, entering the specific program goals you have
formulated to maintain or enhance your strengths, or to address identified challenges. In
writing your objectives and developing your resource requests, consider student learning
and program assessment results. Assign an overall priority to each goal and each
objective. In addition, enter any actions and/or resources required to achieve each
objective. Then complete a CQIP for each new budget allocation request.
As has been demonstrated throughout this program review, great strides have been made to
establish consistency in instruction, performance, assessment, and growth. Increased enrollment,
better retention and student success, intentional outreach, as well as having two full-time
instructors, has led to an increase in stability and quality throughout the program. Dialogue
between the two full time instructors has led to a vision including new ensembles, certificate
programs, articulation agreements with four-year institutions, and a more vibrant, energetic, and
dynamic music department.
Moving forward, over the next four years, the intent is to enact the goals set out in the outline
shown in pages 100-101. In addition, we need to identify, and secure quality EIAs to provide
instruction for music majors beyond solely vocal majors, we need to secure a full-time staff
accompanist/instructor to be shared between the music and theatre departments, and we need to
re-establish budgets that have been zeroed out in order to serve our students in a meaningful and
relevant way, that is competitive with other Community College music programs throughout the
California as well as the southern region of Oregon. The last four years were spent building a
